coredata.dc:title:
Application of
H
∞
-optimal controllers for battery-based bidirectional DC/DC converters in hybrid energy storage systems

coredata.dc:creator: Meng, Jianwen
Guo, Qihao
Lin, Jianheng
Yue, Meiling
Diallo, Demba
coredata.dc:description:
Lithium-ion battery-based hybrid energy storage systems (ESSs) have been widely applied in various fields. Bidirectional DC/DC converters, crucial interfaces linking batteries and DC buses, serve as critical actuators for tasks such as DC bus regulation, on-line battery diagnosis, health-conscious energy management strategy, and fault tolerant control. Despite their importance, the control of bidirectional DC/DC converters in hybrid ESSs has rarely been independently discussed. Their control-related works are usually carried out by the unidirectional DC/DC converter modeling process with proportional–integral (PI)-type controllers. Therefore, recognizing the significance of pulsed power loads and the induced highly dynamic changes in battery voltage during real-world applications, this paper highlights the relevance of applying
H
∞
-optimal controllers for bidirectional DC/DC converters based on its detailed control-oriented modeling process. To present a rigorous
H
∞
-optimal controller synthesis process, this study includes a detailed experimental determination of the controller’s key parameters. Furthermore, extensive numerical tests across diverse battery operation scenarios have been conducted to demonstrate the advantages of applying
H
∞
-optimal controllers for battery-based bidirectional DC/DC converters in hybrid ESSs.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Lithium-ion battery-based hybrid energy storage systems (ESSs) have been widely applied in various fields. Bidirectional DC/DC converters, crucial interfaces linking batteries and DC buses, serve as critical actuators for tasks such as DC bus regulation, on-line battery diagnosis, health-conscious energy management strategy, and fault tolerant control. Despite their importance, the control of bidirectional DC/DC converters in hybrid ESSs has rarely been independently discussed. Their control-related works are usually carried out by the unidirectional DC/DC converter modeling process with proportional–integral (PI)-type controllers. Therefore, recognizing the significance of pulsed power loads and the induced highly dynamic changes in battery voltage during real-world applications, this paper highlights the relevance of applying
H
∞
-optimal controllers for bidirectional DC/DC converters based on its detailed control-oriented modeling process. To present a rigorous
H
∞
-optimal controller synthesis process, this study includes a detailed experimental determination of the controller’s key parameters. Furthermore, extensive numerical tests across diverse battery operation scenarios have been conducted to demonstrate the advantages of applying
H
∞
-optimal controllers for battery-based bidirectional DC/DC converters in hybrid ESSs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors are unable or have chosen not to specify which data has been used.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction
ce:section.ce:para: Energy storage systems (ESSs) refer to equipment that can store and release energy stably in a safe manner [1]. Due to the complementary characteristics of different ESS devices in terms of power and energy density, life cycle, response rate, etc., hybrid ESSs become state-of-the-art power sources recently [2]. Combining the advantages of a single energy storage device through power electronic interfacing circuits, hybrid ESSs can meet the desirable requirements simultaneously without fundamental development of the storage mechanism [3]. Actually, hybrid ESSs play a critical role during the process of achieving carbon neutrality. Because electrical energy storage is indispensable and required in various applications [4].

For instance, researchers combat climate change using renewable energy, but the intermittent nature of sources like wind and solar poses challenges [5]. To address this, the integration of promising renewable power generation technologies with ESSs is mandatory. ESSs regulate output, ensuring a stable power supply by smoothing fluctuations and enhancing continuity and quality [6]. In addition, the challenges of the energy crisis, environmental issues, and political concerns drive the need for alternative fuel-driven vehicles in the electrified transport sector [7]. For example, electric vehicles (EVs) are experiencing significant growth, with ESSs playing a vital role in the powertrain. ESSs support the propulsion system, influencing driving mileage, dynamic performance, and energy efficiency. Additionally, ESSs enable the reuse of regenerative energy from braking, benefiting various transport modes including road, rail, maritime, and air. Furthermore, to achieve sustainable greenhouse gas levels in buildings, photovoltaic (PV) panels, micro-turbine, and ESSs are combined together to cut non-renewable energy demand [8]. The microgrid, linked with EV charging infrastructure, can connect to or operate independently from the main grid [9]. ESSs play a vital role in both modes, shaving load peaks in connected mode and stabilizing the microgrid in islanded mode [10].

Especially, lithium-ion battery-based hybrid ESSs have been widely accepted [11]. Because lithium-ion cells are characterized by low self-discharge rate, high energy/power density, reduced memory effect, superior lifespan, and stable electrochemical property [12]. Therefore, usually interfaced with bidirectional DC/DC converters, lithium-ion battery-based hybrid ESSs can be access-oriented storage devices that supply short-term power needs, or capacity-oriented storage devices that meet long-term energy needs [13]. The common objective is to maintain a stable DC bus voltage to realize the purpose of plug-and-play [14].

Over time, lithium-ion cells undergo an unavoidable aging process attributed to the depletion of lithium inventory and the loss of active anode/cathode materials [15]. Consequently, research topics focusing on battery health-conscious control and energy management have gained significant attention recently. As crucial interfaces for lithium-ion batteries, the associated bidirectional DC/DC converters play a vital role in battery on-line state monitoring, fault diagnosis, cell balancing, charging control, modular and reconfigurable concepts, and fault-tolerant control. In [16], an online continuous battery impedance monitoring method is proposed, utilizing the high-frequency electromagnetic oscillations excited by the pulse width modulation switching of a DC/DC converter in a lithium-ion battery system. The estimated battery impedance serves as a crucial indicator to evaluate the battery’s state-of-health (SOH). In [17], a diagnosis method of broadband battery impedance spectroscopy is proposed. The excitation signal is injected through the inner feedback loop of a DC/DC boost converter with average current-mode control. A similar research idea can be applied to the early detection of micro internal short circuit cells, as presented in [18]. In [19], a hierarchical active balancing architecture for series-connected lithium-ion batteries is proposed, based on the control of a multidirectional multiport converter. Using this architecture, a balancing control utilizing the state-of-charge (SOC) of each cell is proposed to achieve balance across all cells. In [20], a flexible power tracking algorithm for PV-battery energy systems is proposed. The battery, controlled by the non-isolated DC/DC converter, can limit the charging and discharging current based on the battery’s SOH. This capability envisions an extended battery service life. In [21], a novel modular, reconfigurable battery energy storage system is proposed. The design is characterized by tightly integrated reconfigurable power switches and DC/DC converters. This feature enables the isolation of faulty cells from the system and allows precise power control for individual cells, contributing to optimal system-level performance.

As the importance of battery-interfaced bidirectional DC/DC converters has been clearly emphasized, control-oriented research has never stopped and continues to be a subject of ongoing discussions. Several representative works are reviewed briefly as follows. The classical proportional–integral (PI)-type controllers with the voltage-and-current double-loop control structure are the most commonly used control strategy, as presented in [22]. This control structure is also the most integrated into the scope of battery-related system control and energy management strategies (EMSs). Nevertheless, the gains of the PID controllers are typically determined under specific test conditions. Applying these values to different conditions often leads to unsatisfactory performance under varying operating scenarios. In addition, model predictive control (MPC), which uses a predefined cost function to make system variables follow their reference values, is widely discussed in bidirectional DC/DC converters for battery applications. For instance, based on the MPC method, a lithium-ion battery fast active balancing strategy is proposed in [23], providing an accurate optimal balancing current. However, the online calculation burden remains a significant challenge in implementing MPC at the commercial level. Particularly, achieving better control results often requires a longer prediction horizon [11]. Furthermore, the sliding mode controller, a recognized nonlinear control technique, has been explored for bidirectional DC/DC converters in hybrid ESSs. This large-signal-based method effectively addresses the nonlinear control problem with matched uncertainties. In [24], the sliding mode controller is employed for the inner current loop control of a classical bidirectional DC/DC converter. The control outcomes are compared with PI-type controllers, revealing a significant improvement in tightly regulated DC bus voltage. Moreover, considering the nonlinear and uncertain characteristics of bidirectional DC/DC converters in the context of hybrid ESSs, fuzzy logic control has become very popular. This is particularly relevant due to the wide-ranging variations in the battery charging and discharging process. Therefore, fuzzy logic controllers are widely used in bidirectional DC/DC converters. For example, in [25], an intelligent scheme employing fuzzy logic is applied to monitor the SOC of the batteries and determine the reference current for current control through the bidirectional DC–DC converter in a multi-microgrid system. Last but not least, data-driven model-free control techniques have recently been discussed for the DC/DC converters. For example, a deep reinforcement learning-based control law has been proposed in [26], where the algorithm can interact with the environment to learn and control without prior knowledge.

Although various control laws have been explored for battery-interfaced bidirectional DC/DC converter control, further discussion is needed, as highlighted in the recent comprehensive review in [27]. A notable challenge in real applications involves pulsed power loads, which impacts the DC bus voltage dynamics and hybrid ESSs’ power quality [28,29]. Furthermore, previous research works have overlooked two key aspects. Firstly, modeling of bidirectional DC/DC converters is often simplified to unidirectional, neglecting bidirectional power flow. Secondly, conventional PI-type controllers dominate EMS-oriented power converter control, overlooking pulsed power load characteristics and induced battery voltage changes. Therefore, motivated by these factors, this paper contributes by employing
H
∞
-optimal controllers for battery-based bidirectional DC/DC converters. This research aligns with current trends in battery health-conscious control-oriented works.

This paper is structured as follows. Section 2 provides a detailed overview of the bidirectional DC/DC power converter under study and its control-oriented modeling process. Section 3 introduces the dual-loop control structure based on our recent experimental comparative study [30]. The controller design process, covering PI-type controllers and
H
∞
-optimal controllers, is also presented in Section 3. The experimental tuning process of the used controllers is presented in Section 4. Additionally, Section 5 shows numerical tests of applying
H
∞
-optimal controllers across diverse battery operation scenarios. Finally, Section 6 concludes the paper.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bidirectional DC/DC power converter modeling
Topology of the studied bidirectional power stage
ce:section.ce:para:
The topology of the non-isolated bidirectional DC/DC power converter under study is illustrated in Fig. 1. As the current flow in power switches is unidirectional, the bidirectional power stage is commonly achieved by employing two semiconductor power switches in parallel, each with a diode [31,32]. These two semiconductor power switches operate at the frequency

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dual-loop DC/DC power converter control structure and controller design
ce:section.ce:para:
The voltage-and-current dual-loop DC/DC power converter control structure is illustrated in Fig. 3. The voltage controller is in the outer loop, while the current controller is in the inner loop. In the context of hybrid ESSs, the control objective is to maintain the output voltage
v
H
at the desired reference value
v
ref
despite disturbances from the lower voltage side
v
L
and the characteristics of pulsed power loads. As detailed in our recent experimental comparative study [30], the dual-loop control structure is retained because of its superior performance compared to the single-voltage-loop control structure. Especially, this control structure allows for the control of the inductor current, thereby mitigating the risk of current overshoots. These overshoots can be potentially dangerous, particularly when large voltage steps occur. Thus, the adoption of the voltage-and-current dual-loop control structure not only offers improved performance but also addresses critical safety concerns in dynamic voltage scenarios.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Controller design by shaping open-loop transfer functions
ce:section.ce:para: Loop shaping is a classical controller design method in the frequency domain. Its main idea is to achieve a desired open-loop transfer function with specified critical indicators, such as crossover frequency and phase margin. The open-loop transfer function, which is the product of all the transfer functions around the control loop (including the feedback controller to be designed, the plant, the actuator, and the measurement device), should have the loop gain as large as possible at low-frequency range. Additionally, the loop gain should roll off at least as fast as the plant at the high-frequency range [33]. Typically, no optimization is involved in the design process. This characteristic magnitude behavior in different frequency ranges is desired for reference tracking and disturbance rejection at the low-frequency range, and noise reduction at the high-frequency range.

While shaping the open-loop transfer functions is easy to understand, it does not directly consider closed-loop transfer functions such as sensitivity function, complementary sensitivity function, and noise sensitivity function [33]. These closed-loop sensitivity functions play a crucial role in determining the quality of the system’s final response. Additionally, the classical loop shaping approach does not directly account for the usual disturbances in the system, resulting in relatively poor robustness of the designed controller against exogenous input signals.

ce:section.ce:section-title:
Controller design within
H
∞
-optimal control framework
ce:section.ce:para:
An alternative design method is to directly shape the related closed-loop transfer functions, considering disturbances and weighting factors. The feedback controller design problem is then formulated as an
H
∞
-optimization problem, requiring a general control configuration for the standard problem formulation [33].

As shown in Fig. 6, a general control configuration adapted to the specific control problem in this paper is given. The augmented plant P consists of the process G to be controlled, the disturbance mapping E , the weighting factor
W
1
for sensitivity function, the weighting factor
W
2
for noise sensitivity function, and the weighting factor
W

for complementary sensitivity function. Additionally, w includes the exogenous inputs such as disturbance f and reference input r ; while η includes the weighted outputs to be minimized, such as
η

η =

H
∞

In general, reaching the global minimum solution

within a given tolerance [33].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental determination of reference crossover frequency and weighting factors
ce:section.ce:para: To understand the dynamic behavior of the system and design an effective controller with appropriately tuned weighting factors, the crossover frequency plays a pivotal role. Therefore, it is necessary to experimentally determine the reference crossover frequency. The experimental setup, illustrated in Fig. 7, includes a power switch module driven by the ARCAL 2106 driver board from ARCEL. Additionally, the real-time control platform employs a dSPACE MicroLabBox 1202 with a sampling time of 50 μ s. Furthermore, a DELTA ELEKTRONIKA SM500-CP-90 controllable voltage source is used to emulate the DC input voltage. The configuration of the test bench is resumed in Table 1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reference crossover frequency determination for the inner current control loop
ce:section.ce:para: With the previously presented PI-type current controller, the reference crossover frequency for the inner control loop is initially fixed. To this end, four PI-type inner current loop controllers are designed with crossover frequencies of 2000 Hz, 1500 Hz, 1000 Hz, and 500 Hz, respectively, based on the switching frequency of the power switches in use. The reference inductor current value is set to 8 A, aligned with the nominal value specified by the electrical characteristics of the test bench.

After entering the steady-state response, a 10% increase in the DC input side voltage is introduced. The resulting change in the DC input side voltage is depicted in Fig. 8(a). Furthermore, Fig. 8(b) and (c) illustrate the dynamic behaviors of the inductor current and the duty cycle, respectively.

Additionally, statistical information about the inner control loop crossover frequency determination is listed in Table 2. The signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) in dB for duty cycle and controlled current in the steady-state phase is calculated and compared for the four tested crossover frequencies. The SNR is calculated by
SNR = 10 ×
log
10
(
μ
s i g n a l
2
σ
n o i s e
2
)
, where
μ
s i g n a l
is the mean of the non-negative signal, and
σ
n o i s e
is the noise standard deviation. Moreover, the current perturbation deviation in % , defined as the ratio between the maximum deviated value and the reference value, is calculated and compared, as listed in Table 2.

It is evident from these figures and statistical analysis that a higher crossover frequency ensures tight control of the inductor current but comes at the expense of introducing more noise into the control loop. Therefore, considering the compromise between the dynamic behavior and noise reduction, a crossover frequency of 1000 Hz is selected for the inner current control loop.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reference crossover frequency determination for the outer voltage control loop
ce:section.ce:para: In this phase, with the previously fixed inner control loop crossover frequency, we focus on determining the reference crossover frequency for the outer voltage control loop. Four PI-type outer voltage loop controllers are designed with crossover frequencies of 200 Hz, 150 Hz, 100 Hz, and 50 Hz, respectively. The reference output voltage is set to 48 V. The inner current loop is controlled by the previously fixed PI-type controller with a crossover frequency of 1000 Hz.

Similarly, after entering the steady-state response, a 10% increase from the nominal value is introduced to the DC input side voltage. The resulting change in the DC input side voltage is shown in Fig. 9(a). The dynamic behaviors of the inductor current, the duty cycle, and the controlled output voltage are depicted in Fig. 9(b), (c), and (d), respectively. As observed in Fig. 9(d), a higher crossover frequency can ensure tight control of the output voltage. However, as shown in Fig. 9(b) and (c), the response of the inner control loop is very sensitive to the higher outer control loop crossover frequency. Specifically, a higher outer control loop crossover frequency can introduce more measurement noise for the inner control loop.

As previously analyzed, the statistical information about the determination of the outer control loop crossover frequency is listed in Table 3. The SNR in dB for duty cycle and controlled current during the steady-state phase is calculated and compared for the four tested crossover frequencies. Additionally, the voltage perturbation deviation in % for the controlled output voltage is calculated and compared, as listed in Table 3. Therefore, considering the compromise between the dynamic behavior and noise reduction, the crossover frequency of the outer voltage control loop is selected as 50 Hz.

ce:section.ce:section-title:
Frequency-based weighting factors determination for
H
∞
controller synthesis
Inner current loop weighting factors
ce:section.ce:para:
Given the inherent significance of the inner current loop, the initial focus is on determining the frequency-based weighting factors for the synthesis of the inner current
H
∞
controller. This step is critical as it profoundly influences the behavior of the controlled current as presented previously. To establish a fair and meaningful comparison with the classical PI-type controller, the closed-loop bandwidth of the previous PI-type inner loop control system serves as the tuning reference. The closed-loop bandwidth, defined by the complementary sensitivity function, signifies the highest frequency at which the complementary sensitivity function crosses −3 dB from above on the Bode plot [33]. The determined frequency-based weighting factors for the inner current
H
∞
controller synthesis are summarized in Table 4, providing a structured basis for performance evaluation. The comprehensive explanation of the physical significance and interpretation of the parameters associated with these weighting factors can be refereed to Section 3.2.1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Outer voltage loop weighting factors
ce:section.ce:para:
As discussed earlier during the PI-type controller crossover frequency determination process, the outer voltage loop faces the challenge of finding a balance between achieving tight control of the DC bus and minimizing ripple in the controlled inductor current. In particular, the high fluctuating inductor current has been identified as a factor that can induce premature aging of the battery [34]. Therefore, the determination of weighting factors for the outer voltage loop becomes a crucial step. To ensure a relatively fair tuning, we choose the identical noise level in the controlled inductor current between the double PI-type and
H
∞
-optimal control strategies as the tuning reference.

Consequently, the tuned weighting factors for the outer voltage loop are outlined in Table 5. Furthermore, the comparative tests are conducted for the double PI-type and
H
∞
optimal control strategies, as illustrated in Fig. 10. The statistical information about the comparative tests has also been listed in Table 6. During the tests, the inner current
H
∞
controller is synthesized with the determined weighting factors listed in Table 4. The outer voltage
H
∞
controller is synthesized with the weighting factors listed in Table 5.

Initially, in Fig. 10(a), sequential changes of
± 10 %
and
± 20 %
step increase and decrease are applied to the DC input side voltage after the converter system entering the steady-state phase. The controlled inductor current is shown in Fig. 10(b). As it can be observed in Fig. 10(b), the peak value of the controlled inductor current is smaller with the
H
∞
-optimal controller, regardless of the step change directions. Additionally, as shown in Fig. 10(c), the
H
∞
control strategy has significant less voltage perturbation deviation. Finally, although the PI-type controller yielded similar controlled dynamic responses for inductor current and output voltage, a larger peak inductor current value and loosely controlled output voltage are evident. These are mainly attributed to the non-optimal design process of PI-type controllers, where the DC input side voltage disturbance is not considered.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Numerical tests across diverse battery operation scenarios
ce:section.ce:para:
In this section,
H
∞
-optimal controllers for bidirectional DC/DC converter control will be demonstrated on a battery-based ESS employed in a hybrid EV powertrain. Diverse battery operation scenarios will be considered, under which the advantages of applying
H
∞
-optimal controllers will be shown.

The hybrid EV powertrain under study, simplified from an experimentally validated two-driven-wheels hybrid EV tested under real driving conditions around the Université de Lille [35], is shown in Fig. 11(a). In particular, we focus on the lithium-ion battery-based hybrid ESS. The hybrid ESS is composed of a proton exchange membrane fuel cell (PEMFC) serving as the primary energy source, along with a lithium-ion battery pack operating as the secondary energy source. Furthermore, these two energy sources are managed by a simple thermostat (on/off) strategy. Specifically, the vehicle’s power demand is met by the sum of the power output from the PEMFC and the battery, and the battery’s SOC is maintained by the PEMFC within the range of
60 % ∼ 80 %
.

The required current from the battery side for DC bus voltage regulation is depicted in Fig. 11(b). The positive current value indicates the battery’s discharging process, while the negative current value indicates the battery’s charging process. As observed in Fig. 11(b), the required current from the battery side is highly dynamic. Moreover, the lithium-ion battery’s voltage variation under the dynamic current excitation is shown in Fig. 11(c). Due to the highly dynamic required battery output current, the battery’s voltage variation is also highly dynamic.

As a crucial interface between the lithium-ion battery and DC bus, the control of bidirectional DC/DC converters plays a critical role in the application of battery-based hybrid ESSs. The typical pulsed power load characteristic and the induced input side battery voltage change cannot be ignored [27]. Therefore, an optimal bidirectional DC/DC power converter control strategy that considers these realistic disturbance is relevant.

To focus on the discussion of the advantages brought by
H
∞
-optimal controllers across diverse battery operation scenarios, we retain an experimentally validated electro-thermal cylindrical lithium-ion cell model from [36] in the following discussion. The electrical and thermal characteristics of this electro-thermal cell model can be found in [37,38]. Additionally, numerical simulation tests adopt a 7S1P (seven cells in series and one cell in parallel) battery pack structure, taking into account the nominal value of the lower voltage side in the bidirectional DC/DC converter under study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Test for healthy battery operation scenario
ce:section.ce:para:
Firstly, the test results under the healthy battery operation scenario are presented. The comparative results between PI-type and
H
∞
-optimal controllers during a real driving cycle are depicted in Fig. 12. The required current from battery side for DC bus regulation matches the data shown in Fig. 11(b). The bidirectional DC/DC converter remains consistent with the experimental setup, while the battery voltage and the DC bus voltage to be regulated are set based on the characteristics of the experimental test bench.

As shown in Fig. 12(a), a notable advantage of applying
H
∞
-optimal controllers for bidirectional DC/DC converters in the context of battery-based hybrid ESSs is a more tightly regulated DC bus voltage. However, in the case of healthy battery operation as shown in Fig. 12(b), the battery side inductor current controlled by
H
∞
-optimal controllers remains nearly identical to that controlled by PI-type controllers, irrespective of the battery charging or discharging process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Test for modular and reconfigurable battery scenario
ce:section.ce:para: Secondly, the test results for the battery under a modular and reconfigurable scenario are presented. Due to the uneven temperature distribution of the battery pack, the cells inevitably exhibit different states such as SOC, SOH, and state-of-power (SOP). Furthermore, considering the topic of battery online diagnosis for over-charge, over-discharge, incipient short circuit, etc., the modular and reconfigurable battery system is critical for reliability engineering and system safety [39].

Therefore, the simulation tests involving the modular and reconfigurable battery system are relevant. As presented previously, the battery pack used in the simulation test has a 7S1P structure. During the simulation tests, a battery cell will be disconnected at the 40th second and re-inserted into the battery pack at the 120th second to assess the performance of
H
∞
-optimal controllers for batteries under a modular and reconfigurable scenario.

The comparative results between PI-type and
H
∞
-optimal controllers during a real driving cycle are depicted in Fig. 13. As shown in Fig. 13(a), during the two-times battery reconfigurable process, the
H
∞
-optimal controllers can effectively suppress the DC bus voltage variation induced by the changed battery structure. Additionally, the control inductor current is depicted in Fig. 13(b), revealing similar dynamics between PI-type and
H
∞
-optimal controllers. However,
H
∞
-optimal controllers exhibit a smaller short-time peak value in the controlled current.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Test for abnormal ohmic resistance variation scenario
ce:section.ce:para:
Thirdly, the test results under the scenario of abnormal ohmic resistance variation inside battery are presented. The battery’s ohmic resistance is an important indicator for evaluating the battery’s SOH; however, it changes due to factors such as temperature, current direction, SOC interval, and aging process [40]. Therefore, conducting a tolerance control test against changes in the battery’s ohmic resistance is also relevant in the context of hybrid ESSs. The comparative outcomes between PI-type and
H
∞
-optimal controllers are shown in Fig. 14. The sequential variation of ohmic resistance by
± 50 %
of its average value is shown in Fig. 14(a), introduced in the simulation tests starting from the 5th second. Subsequently, Fig. 14(b) highlights the robust characteristic of
H
∞
-optimal controllers in tolerating abnormal ohmic resistance variations within the battery. Specifically, the
H
∞
-optimal controllers demonstrate their effectiveness in reducing the fluctuation of DC bus voltage associated with abnormal ohmic resistance variations. Moreover, as shown in Fig. 14(c), in the context of the battery’s abnormal ohmic resistance variation, the battery side inductor current controlled by
H
∞
-optimal controllers exhibits a smaller peak value compared to that controlled by PI-type controllers. This is of great importance as it helps prevent further battery deterioration caused by high fluctuating inductor currents.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Test for battery thermal management default scenario
ce:section.ce:para:
Finally, the test results conducted under the default scenario of battery thermal management system are presented. Battery thermal management is an important topic, as it significantly influences the battery’s electrical performance and safety. Therefore, the performance of
H
∞
-optimal controllers in tolerating default conditions in battery thermal management is discussed here. Fig. 15 illustrates the comparative outcomes between PI-type and
H
∞
-optimal controllers. Specifically, Fig. 15(a) depicts the simulated sequential variation of battery surface temperature, ranging from
± 50 %
of its average value, initiated in the simulation tests starting from the 5th second.

Furthermore, Fig. 15(b) emphasizes the robust nature of
H
∞
- optimal controllers in handling abnormal temperature variations on the battery surface. Significantly, these controllers demonstrate their effectiveness in minimizing the fluctuation of the DC bus voltage linked to simulated temperature variations. Moreover, Fig. 15(c) reveals that, within the context of the battery’s thermal management default scenario, the battery side inductor current controlled by
H
∞
-optimal controllers exhibits a smaller peak value compared to that controlled by PI-type controllers. This holds significant importance in preventing potential battery thermal runaway [41].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusion
ce:section.ce:para:
In the context of lithium-ion battery-based hybrid energy storage systems, the application of
H
∞
-optimal controllers for bidirectional DC/DC converters has been presented in this paper. The voltage-and-current dual-loop control structure is retained based on our recent experimental comparative study. The bidirectional power flow characteristic of the converter is analytically presented during the detailed control-oriented modeling process. Two controller design processes, namely PI-type and
H ∞
-optimal controllers, are explained as a design guide. Experimental determination of the controller’s key parameters has also been presented to guarantee a reasonable comparative study. The reference crossover frequency for PI-type controllers is selected with a compromise between dynamic behavior and high frequency noise reduction. Frequency-based weighting factors for the synthesis of
H
∞
-optimal controllers are fixed by referring to the closed-loop bandwidth of PI-type control systems. Numerical tests are conducted across four battery operation scenarios to demonstrate the advantages of applying
H
∞
-optimal controllers. These scenarios include battery under healthy operation, battery with modular and reconfigurable structure, battery under abnormal ohmic resistance variation, and battery under thermal management default.
H
∞
-optimal controllers consistently outperform commonly used PI-type controllers in all proposed test conditions, showing tightly regulated DC bus voltage and smaller peak values of controlled inductor current. These research findings hold significant importance for battery reliability and safety engineering.
